exemplar.title: The Culture one is brought up in as a predictor of how anger will be expressed

detailedExemplarData.title: The Culture one is brought up in as a predictor of how anger will be expressed

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and effectively, with clear signposting throughout.
Research question is clearly stated, focused, and consistently connected to the discussion.
Headings and structural signposts reinforce sustained focus on predicting anger expression from culture.
weaknesses: Methodology description lacks transparencyâsearch strategy, databases, and inclusion/exclusion criteria are not detailed.
Occasional imprecise phrasing slightly blunts clarity (e.g., âSeparate which is whenâ¦â).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (topic communication and research question) comfortably meet the top band, while methodology shows only partial evidence of systematic selection, placing it in the 3â4 band. An overall score of 5 reflects strong focus and purpose with minor methodological omissions.

strengths: Excellent integration of classic and contemporary psychological theories with accurate empirical details.
Consistent and accurate use of subject-specific terminology and concepts throughout.
Interdisciplinary breadth demonstrated by combining cultural and genetic research sources.
weaknesses: Occasional omission of statistical specifics (effect sizes, p-values) in study summaries.
Minor terminology slip (e.g., using âSeparateâ instead of âseparationâ), and incomplete reporting of quality-control steps (HardyâWeinberg checks).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and understanding of subject matter and terminology is overall excellent, meriting the top band, but minor gaps in statistical detail and procedural reporting prevent a flawless performance. A score of 5 is therefore appropriate.

strengths: Broad and purposeful selection of relevant studies, with clear links to the research question.
Analysis moves beyond descriptionâevaluating methodological strengths/limitations and identifying biases.
Demonstrates critical awareness of key issues such as social-desirability bias and geneâculture co-evolution.
weaknesses: Discussion lacks depth of contradictory evidence and cohesive theoretical synthesis.
Quantitative metrics (effect sizes, model-fit indices) are largely absent, weakening analytical rigor.
Proposals for future research remain general rather than specific or innovative.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality and analysis are solidly within the 7â9 band, but evaluation is uneven and statistical rigor is light. An overall Critical Thinking score of 7 reflects good, mostly well-supported analysis with room for deeper critical evaluation.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure with title page, table of contents, numbered headings, and logical progression.
Consistent formattingâword count stated, page numbers present, uniform citation style.
Comprehensive table of contents and effective preview paragraph enhance navigability.
weaknesses: Minor misalignments between section headings and page numbers in the table of contents.
Occasional uneven subsection numbering and small formatting inconsistencies (broken links, italics).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and formatting adhere to IB conventions and facilitate reader navigation, but small lapses in alignment and consistency prevent a top-band rating. A score of 3 accurately reflects this performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical languageâdisplay rules, acculturation strategies, 5-HTTLPR polymorphism, bidirectional ambiguityâis used accurately and consistently.  Definitions are provided on first use, and terminology is applied correctly when analysing each study.  Occasional slips (e.g., âSeparateâ instead of âseparationâ) are minor and do not impede understanding.  The sustained precision and appropriateness merit the highest band.

Formatting is largely consistent: word count stated, page numbers present, and in-text citations follow a uniform style.  The reference list includes DOIs/URLs, though a few broken links and inconsistent italics persist.  Paragraphs occasionally run long, but spacing, font, and heading hierarchy meet expectations.  These small lapses keep the work within, but not at the top of, the highest presentation band.

Across the essay the student demonstrates excellent command of psychological and cross-cultural literature.  Classic theories (Hofstede, Berry, Tajfel) and contemporary genetic research are integrated, and empirical details such as sample sizes, instruments (STAXI, BPAQ) and gene polymorphisms are accurately reported.  Sources are well chosen to illuminate each thematic strand.  Minor gapsâe.g., occasional omission of statistical specifics and over-reliance on descriptive summariesâprevent a flawless demonstration, but the overall depth and consistency of application align with the top band.

The essay outlines broad methodological choices (predominant reliance on questionnaires due to ethical limits in anger research) and comments on social-desirability bias, but it stops short of detailing search strategies, inclusion / exclusion criteria, or justification for the final set of sources.  While the chosen studies are clearly relevant and varied, there is only partial evidence that their selection followed a deliberate, systematic process; databases, key terms, and decision points are not documented.  This places the work firmly in the âmostly completeâ band, as methodology is present and generally appropriate yet lacks full transparency.

The student presents the topic immediately, defines anger and culture, and explains the real-world importance of studying cultural variation in anger.  The introduction is concise yet detailed, making the focus of the investigation clear, and each subsequent section is briefly previewed so the reader understands why every theme is relevant.  Throughout the body, headings and signposting consistently bring discussion back to the core issue of predicting anger expression from culture.  Minor lapses in phrasing (e.g., âSeparate which is whenâ¦â) very occasionally blunt precision, but overall the topic, purpose, and focus are communicated accurately and effectively, meeting the top-band description.

The student selects a broad yet coherent set of peer-reviewed articles spanning cross-cultural surveys, acculturation research, and behavioural genetics.  Each study clearly links to the research question; irrelevant or anecdotal material is absent.  Source variety (32-nation survey, child samples, immigrant samples, multi-ethnic genetic work) shows purposeful breadth.  Minor limitationsâheavy reliance on self-report measures and concentration on a few geographical clustersâprevent a judgment of âexcellentâ consistency, situating the work solidly in the âgood / mostly relevantâ band.

The research questionââTo what extent does the culture one is raised in predict how anger is expressed?ââis explicitly stated, narrow, and repeatedly referenced when evaluating each cluster of studies.  The sub-sections (display rules, acculturation, genetics) all map directly onto this question, demonstrating sustained focus.  No drift into tangential material is observed; even biological findings are tied back to cultural prediction of anger.  Hence the RQ is clearly stated, well-focused, and effectively connected to the ensuing discussion, warranting the highest band.

The essay adheres to IB EE conventions: title page, abstract-like introduction, table of contents, numbered headings, and reference list.  Logical progression from introduction through discussion to conclusion is evident.  Minor flawsâuneven subsection numbering and occasional abrupt transitionsâslightly hinder navigation but do not undermine overall coherence, so the structure remains clearly appropriate.

The discussion section synthesises display-rule, acculturation, and genetic evidence into a reasoned argument that culture is a strong, though not exclusive, predictor of anger expression.  Practical implications for education and mental-health contexts are articulated, and methodological caveats (social desirability, lack of causality) are evaluated.  Critical engagement is present but uneven: contradictory findings are scarce, and future research proposals remain general.  This reflects a well-reasoned argument with partial critical evaluation, aligning with the 7-9 bandâs lower end.

The essay moves beyond description to compare findings across cultural frameworks, acculturation strategies, and genetic correlates.  Strengths and limitations of each study (sample size, self-report bias, bidirectional ambiguity) are explicitly analysed, and conclusions are generally supported by evidence.  Nevertheless, statistical depth is lightâeffect sizes and p-values are rarely citedâand competing interpretations (e.g., conflating aggression with anger) receive only brief mention.  Thus the analysis is clear and mostly supported but not yet at the âexcellentâ threshold.

annotations.comment: The table of contents is comprehensive and accurately maps each section to its page, facilitating easy navigation.
Minor misalignment between section headings and page numerals in the table of contents could be tightened to improve precision in navigation.
In summarising Novin et al., the operationalization of intrapersonal coping strategies is vague; include details on coding procedures and measurement scales.
The research question is not explicitly stated in this opening paragraph; consider introducing it earlier to strengthen focus and signposting.
The research question is clearly and prominently stated in bold, anchoring the investigation with precise focus.
The summary of cultural differences lacks quantitative metrics (such as effect sizes or p-values); reporting these would substantiate analytical claims.
The Hallak study description clearly delineates participant groups and employs the STAXI scale, strengthening methodological clarity.
The discussion acknowledges bidirectional ambiguity and the co-evolution of genes and culture, reflecting high-level critical thinking.
The OXTR polymorphism discussion overlooks potential geneâenvironment interactions and socio-cultural moderators; consider integrating GÃE frameworks.
Structural equation modelling is reported without model-fit indices (e.g., CFI, RMSEA, ÏÂ²/df); include these to substantiate analytical rigor.
The genetic analysis omits mention of HardyâWeinberg equilibrium checks and correction for multiple comparisons; include these quality-control steps.
Potential confounders such as discrimination experiences or socioeconomic status are not controlled for; address these moderating variables.
Integration of genetic polymorphism research with cultural analysis demonstrates interdisciplinary breadth and depth of source selection.
The title is clearly stated at the start of the document, immediately conveying the focus of the investigation and meeting the requirement for a well-defined title page.
The word count is provided and falls within the maximum limit, demonstrating adherence to formatting guidelines.
The introduction offers clear theoretical definitions of emotions and anger, demonstrating solid subject knowledge and contextual grounding.
The preview paragraph effectively outlines the three major thematic strands, enhancing the structural coherence of the essay.
The methodological rationale notes questionnaire use but omits a description of search strategy, databases and inclusion/exclusion criteria; include these for full transparency.
Use of subject-specific terminology (e.g. amplification, deamplification, masking) in summarising Matsumotoâs display-rules study is accurate and consistent.
The student correctly identifies social-desirability bias as a limitation of self-report surveys, demonstrating critical awareness of methodological constraints.
Terminology slip: uses âSeparateâ instead of the established acculturation term âseparationâ; ensure consistency with standard conceptual definitions.
Cross-cultural comparisons assume STAXI has equivalent validity across groups; discuss measurement invariance and cultural adaptation of the scale.
Aggression and anger are distinct constructs; justify the use of the Buss-Perry Aggression Questionnaire as a proxy for anger expression.
The discussion section largely reiterates study summaries without developing a cohesive theoretical model; deepen synthesis around the research question.
